design_philosophy.core_loop: Qualify competitively, manage car setup across practice/qualifying/race sessions, execute precise driving over multiple lap distances while managing fuel, tire wear, and collision damage
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing aggressive racing pace against physical car damage and mechanical reliability; tension between player skill and realistic simulation systems
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Authentic Indianapolis 500 participant—compete against accurate 1989 grid field in full competitive simulation with realistic car physics and damage modeling
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneering commitment to racing simulation over arcade accessibility; deep car setup system where tuning directly affects handling; realistic damage and retirement systems; first-person perspective only during racing; strict yellow flag physics and speed restrictions; player car cannot suffer random mechanical failures (player-specific advantage in otherwise simulation-accurate environment)

difficulty.checkpointing: Pit stop-based mid-race adjustments; no checkpoint restart—single race completion is mandatory
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Four selectable race distances (10/30/60/200-lap) with progressive damage systems; 10-lap and 30-lap races disable car damage; 60+ lap races enable collision and mechanical damage. Practice mode allows unlimited risk-free testing. Qualifying is optional but results in back-of-grid starting position. Three car choices with different default setups but equivalent competitive viability with proper tuning. Difficulty emerges from simulation accuracy rather than artificial rubber-banding.

progression.upgrade_system: Real-time car setup modification system: F3-F10 menus control turboboost, anti-roll bars, tire pressure, suspension geometry, gearing, and other racing parameters. Changes immediate in Practice mode; limited to pit stops during Qualifying/Race except turboboost and anti-roll bars
progression.unlock_mechanism: None; all three car variants (Penske-Chevrolet, Lola-Buick, March-Cosworth) available from start
replayability_hooks: Six camera angles in replay mode including Leader/Crash tracking
Three distinct car setups with different handling characteristics
Practice mode for unlimited setup experimentation without race consequences
Multiple race distance options (10/30/60/200-lap) with progressive mechanical damage systems
Qualifying session affects grid position, creating variable starting conditions

world_building_techniques: Authentic 1989 Indianapolis 500 starting grid featuring real qualifying order (with player car #17 replacing Rich Vogler's #29)
Realistic computer-controlled car behavior: crashes, mechanical retirements (bearing, clutch, CV joint, engine, gearbox, ignition, stalled, valve, vibration, radiator, oil leak issues)
Environmental detail: yellow flag system with appropriate speed restrictions (~90 mph during cautions vs ~230 mph race pace), crash debris persistence, pit stop mechanics
Career context: player competes within authentic racing simulation framework with no narrative overlay

danger_signals: Car damage accumulation: wheel damage degrades handling; two tire losses make recovery 'extremely difficult'; rear impact engine damage is unrecoverable
Yellow flag conditions restrict passing and force speed reduction, creating vulnerable racing windows
Computer-controlled crash participation creates dynamic track hazards; wreckage persists 2-3 laps before clearance
Mechanical retirement conditions for AI cars (10 failure types) create variable field compositions across race distances
environmental_storytelling: Indianapolis Motor Speedway track layout with turn-specific physics
Pit stop infrastructure reflects real Indy 500 race operations
Weather-independent consistent conditions emphasize driver and setup skill
Speed differential between caution (90 mph) and race pace (230 mph) visualizes risk/safety tradeoff
